content:
# Exodus Chapter 19 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44r-44v (PDF pp. 84-85); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 60-61 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_019_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_019_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_019_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_019_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE SINAI THEOPHANY AND COVENANT PROPOSAL:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 19 across two pages in standard prose column format. This chapter is the hinge of the entire Torah -- the moment toward which the exodus narrative has been driving and upon which everything that follows depends. YHWH descends onto Mount Sinai in fire, smoke, thunder, and earthquake to establish a covenant with Israel. The chapter moves in five stages: arrival at Sinai (vv. 1-2), the covenant proposal -- the eagle-wing speech (vv. 3-6), the people's acceptance and Moses' mediation (vv. 7-9), consecration instructions and boundary-setting (vv. 10-15), and the theophany itself with its terrifying sensory phenomena (vv. 16-25). The theological density is extraordinary: v. 4 contains the eagle-wings metaphor for divine rescue; v. 5 introduces *segullah* ("treasured possession"), the term for Israel's unique covenantal status; v. 6 presents the dual vocation *mamlechet kohanim vegoy qadosh* ("a kingdom of priests and a holy nation"); vv. 16-19 deploy every sense -- sight (lightning, smoke, fire), sound (thunder, shofar, voice of God), touch (the mountain trembling) -- to convey an event that ruptures all ordinary categories of experience. The boundary-setting (vv. 12-13, 21-24) establishes the foundational principle that the holy and the common cannot casually mix -- approach to God requires preparation, mediation, and limits. Moses' repeated ascents and descents (vv. 3, 7, 8, 14, 20, 25) dramatize his unique role as mediator, the living conduit along the mountain's vertical axis. The Vaticanus LXX text (pp. 60-61) follows the Hebrew closely but introduces significant interpretive shifts, notably rendering *mamlechet kohanim* as *basileion hierateuma* ("royal priesthood") -- a reading that enters the New Testament at 1 Peter 2:9.

---

## Israel Arrives at Sinai (vv. 1-2)

**19:1** In the third month after the going out of the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt, on this day, they came to the wilderness of Sinai. [fn1]

**19:2** And they set out from Rephidim and came to the wilderness of Sinai, and they encamped in the wilderness; and Israel encamped there before the mountain. [fn2]

---

## The Eagle-Wing Speech: Covenant Proposal (vv. 3-6)

**19:3** And Moses went up to God, and YHWH called to him from the mountain, saying, "Thus you shall say to the house of Jacob and declare to the sons of Israel: [fn3]

**19:4** 'You yourselves have seen what I did to Egypt, and I carried you on wings of eagles, and I brought you to myself. [fn4]

**19:5** And now, if you will truly listen to my voice and keep my covenant, then you shall be to me a treasured possession from among all the peoples, for all the earth is mine. [fn5]

**19:6** And you -- you shall be to me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' These are the words that you shall speak to the sons of Israel." [fn6]

---

## The People's Response and Moses' Mediation (vv. 7-9)

**19:7** And Moses came and called for the elders of the people, and he set before them all these words which YHWH had commanded him. [fn7]

**19:8** And all the people answered together and said, "All that YHWH has spoken we will do." And Moses brought back the words of the people to YHWH. [fn8]

**19:9** And YHWH said to Moses, "Behold, I am coming to you in the thickness of the cloud, so that the people may hear when I speak with you, and also in you they may trust forever." And Moses told the words of the people to YHWH. [fn9]

---

## Instructions for Consecration (vv. 10-15)

**19:10** And YHWH said to Moses, "Go to the people and consecrate them today and tomorrow, and let them wash their garments. [fn10]

**19:11** And let them be ready for the third day, for on the third day YHWH will come down before the eyes of all the people upon Mount Sinai. [fn11]

**19:12** And you shall set bounds for the people round about, saying, 'Guard yourselves from going up on the mountain or touching its edge; anyone who touches the mountain shall surely be put to death. [fn12]

**19:13** No hand shall touch him, for he shall surely be stoned or surely be shot through; whether beast or man, he shall not live.' When the ram's horn sounds a long blast, they shall go up to the mountain." [fn13]

**19:14** And Moses went down from the mountain to the people, and he consecrated the people, and they washed their garments. [fn14]

**19:15** And he said to the people, "Be ready for the third day; do not go near a woman." [fn15]

---

## The Theophany on the Third Day (vv. 16-20)

**19:16** And it came to pass on the third day, when it was morning, that there were thunders and lightnings and a heavy cloud upon the mountain, and the sound of a horn exceedingly strong; and all the people who were in the camp trembled. [fn16]

**19:17** And Moses brought out the people from the camp to meet God, and they stationed themselves at the foot of the mountain. [fn17]

**19:18** And Mount Sinai was all of it smoking, because YHWH had descended upon it in fire; and its smoke went up like the smoke of a kiln, and the whole mountain trembled greatly. [fn18]

**19:19** And the sound of the horn was going and growing exceedingly strong; Moses would speak, and God would answer him with a voice. [fn19]

**19:20** And YHWH came down upon Mount Sinai, to the top of the mountain; and YHWH called Moses to the top of the mountain, and Moses went up. [fn20]

---

## The Warning Against Breaking Through (vv. 21-25)

**19:21** And YHWH said to Moses, "Go down, warn the people, lest they break through to YHWH to gaze, and many of them fall." [fn21]

**19:22** And also the priests who draw near to YHWH -- let them consecrate themselves, lest YHWH break out against them." [fn22]

**19:23** And Moses said to YHWH, "The people are not able to go up to Mount Sinai, for you yourself warned us, saying, 'Set bounds about the mountain and consecrate it.'" [fn23]

**19:24** And YHWH said to him, "Go, descend, and come up -- you and Aaron with you; but the priests and the people -- let them not break through to go up to YHWH, lest he break out against them." [fn24]

**19:25** And Moses went down to the people and said to them. [fn25]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Bachodesh hashlishi letset benei-Yisra'el me'erets Mitsrayim bayyom hazzeh ba'u midbar Sinay*: "In the third month...on this day" -- the temporal marker and arrival** -- Hebrew: *bachodesh hashlishi letset benei-Yisra'el me'erets Mitsrayim bayyom hazzeh ba'u midbar Sinay*. The phrase *bayyom hazzeh* ("on this day") is emphatically demonstrative -- *this* day, *that particular* day. Rabbinic tradition debated whether it means the first of the month (Rosh Chodesh Sivan), since no specific date is given (Talmud, Shabbat 86b). The word *chodesh* ("month," from *chadash*, "new") connects time to renewal -- the third new-moon since liberation. The number three becomes thematically important: the third month, the third day (vv. 11, 15, 16) -- threefold preparation before the divine encounter. EMET renders "In the third month of the going out of the sons of Yisra'el from the land of Mitsrayim, on this day." LOGOS renders "In the third month after the going out of the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt, on this day." PNEUMA renders "On the first day of the third month after the Israelites had left Egypt -- on that very day." The consensus follows LOGOS for formal English with standard names (Israel, Egypt), preserving the demonstrative force of *bayyom hazzeh* as "on this day" rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "on that very day." The phrase "sons of Israel" is retained per project convention. LXX: *tou menos tou tritou* ("of the third month") -- straightforward rendering.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Vayyis'u meRefidim vayyavo'u midbar Sinay vayyachanu bammidbar vayyichan-sham Yisra'el neged hahar*: the singular/plural shift -- "as one man with one heart"** -- Hebrew: *vayyis'u meRefidim vayyavo'u midbar Sinay vayyachanu bammidbar vayyichan-sham Yisra'el neged hahar*. The most celebrated grammatical feature of this verse is the shift from plural to singular. The verbs *vayyis'u* ("they set out"), *vayyavo'u* ("they came"), and *vayyachanu* ("they encamped") are all third-person plural. Then the text shifts: *vayyichan-sham Yisra'el* ("Israel encamped there") -- *vayyichan* is third-person *singular*. Rashi's famous comment (citing the Mekhilta): "as one man with one heart" (*ke'ish echad belev echad*). The nation that had been quarreling -- at Rephidim, at Marah, in the wilderness of Sin -- arrives as a plural crowd but encamps as a singular entity. This grammatical unity is the precondition for covenant. The phrase *neged hahar* ("before the mountain" / "facing the mountain") positions Israel as a congregation assembled before the place where heaven and earth will intersect. All three translators preserve the singular/plural shift. EMET renders "they encamped...and Yisra'el encamped." LOGOS renders "they encamped...and Israel encamped." PNEUMA renders "they set up camp...Israel camped." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "they encamped in the wilderness; and Israel encamped there before the mountain" -- preserving the crucial number shift in English. LXX: *kai parenebalen ekei Israel katenanti tou orous* -- preserves the shift.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Koh tomar leveyt Ya'aqov vetagged livnei Yisra'el*: the double address -- "house of Jacob" and "sons of Israel"** -- Hebrew: *uMosheh alah el-ha'Elohim vayyiqra elav YHWH min-hahar le'mor koh tomar leveyt Ya'aqov vetagged livnei Yisra'el*. YHWH instructs Moses to address the people by two names: *beyt Ya'aqov* ("house of Jacob") and *benei Yisra'el* ("sons of Israel"). The Mekhilta interprets the dual naming as addressing the totality of the community. The verbs also differ: *tomar* (from *amar*, "to say") is soft speech; *vetagged* (from *nagad*, "to declare") is forceful proclamation. The same content is communicated gently and firmly -- the diplomacy of covenant invitation. All three translators agree on the double address. EMET renders "the house of Ya'aqov...the sons of Yisra'el." LOGOS renders "the house of Jacob...the sons of Israel." PNEUMA renders "the house of Jacob...the people of Israel." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "the house of Jacob...the sons of Israel" -- retaining "sons of Israel" per project convention rather than PNEUMA's "people of Israel." LXX: *oiko Iakob...huiois Israel* -- preserving the dual address.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Va'essa etkhem al-kanfei nesharim va'avi etkhem elai*: "I carried you on wings of eagles" -- the central metaphor of divine portage** -- Hebrew: *attem re'item asher asiti leMitsrayim va'essa etkhem al-kanfei nesharim va'avi etkhem elai*. Three movements define the verse: (1) *attem re'item* ("you yourselves have seen") -- the emphatic pronoun appeals to Israel's own eyewitness experience; the covenant is grounded in witnessed history, not myth. (2) *va'essa etkhem al-kanfei nesharim* ("I carried you on wings of eagles") -- the *nesher* is most likely the griffon vulture (*Gyps fulvus*), the largest soaring bird in the Levant, with a wingspan exceeding 2.5 meters. The image (developed fully in Deut. 32:11) is not of flight as escape but of flight as intimate divine portage -- a mother bird carrying her young. (3) *va'avi etkhem elai* ("I brought you to myself") -- the destination of the exodus is not a place but a person. God brought Israel to *himself*; Sinai is merely where *elai* ("to me") becomes tangible. EMET renders "wings of nesharim [eagles/vultures]." LOGOS renders "wings of eagles." PNEUMA renders "eagles' wings." All three preserve the core image. The consensus renders "wings of eagles" -- following LOGOS. "Eagles" is retained as the standard English rendering of *nesharim*, with the ornithological note that the referent is likely the griffon vulture preserved in this footnote. The word order "wings of eagles" preserves the Hebrew construct *kanfei nesharim*. LXX: *epi pterugon aeton* ("on wings of eagles") -- identical image.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Segullah mikkol-ha'ammim ki-li kol-ha'arets*: "a treasured possession from among all the peoples" -- the theology of election** -- Hebrew: *ve'attah im-shamoa tishme'u beqoli ushmartem et-beriti vihyitem li segullah mikkol-ha'ammim ki-li kol-ha'arets*. The infinitive absolute *shamoa tishme'u* ("listening you will listen" -- "truly listen") creates emphatic intensification, underscoring the conditionality of the *segullah* status. The word *segullah* (cognate with Akkadian *sikiltu* and Ugaritic *sglt*) denotes a king's personal treasure -- not the national treasury but the private collection the king keeps in his own chamber. Israel is God's personal treasure, set apart from all other peoples as uniquely, intimately his own. The term recurs in Deut. 7:6; 14:2; 26:18; Ps. 135:4; Mal. 3:17; Eccl. 2:8. The clause *ki-li kol-ha'arets* ("for all the earth is mine") is the theological guardrail against triumphalism: God owns all peoples, and Israel's election occurs within -- not against -- his universal sovereignty. The particular serves the universal. EMET renders "a segullah [treasured possession] from all the peoples." LOGOS renders "a treasured possession from among all the peoples." PNEUMA renders "my treasured possession." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "a treasured possession from among all the peoples" -- rendering *segullah* in English while preserving *mikkol-ha'ammim* ("from among all the peoples"), which PNEUMA omits. The conditional "if you will truly listen" renders *shamoa tishme'u* with the emphatic "truly" capturing the infinitive absolute. LXX: *laos periousios apo panton ton ethnon* ("a people of special possession from all the nations") -- *periousios* becomes the basis for Titus 2:14 and 1 Peter 2:9.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Mamlechet kohanim vegoy qadosh*: "a kingdom of priests and a holy nation" -- Israel's dual vocation** -- Hebrew: *ve'attem tihyu-li mamlechet kohanim vegoy qadosh*. Two titles define Israel's vocation. (1) *Mamlechet kohanim* ("kingdom of priests"): the construct chain is deliberately ambiguous -- it can mean a kingdom composed of priests, a kingdom serving a priestly function among the nations, or a priestly body endowed with royal dignity. The consensus renders "kingdom of priests" (NOT "royal priesthood"), preserving the Hebrew construct form *mamlechet* ("kingdom") + *kohanim* ("priests") and its productive ambiguity. The LXX renders *basileion hierateuma* ("royal priesthood"), collapsing the construct into an adjective-noun pair and eliminating the "kingdom" sense -- a rendering adopted at 1 Peter 2:9. The Hebrew "kingdom of priests" is theologically richer because it preserves all three possible readings. (2) *Goy qadosh* ("holy nation"): the use of *goy* rather than *am* is striking. Elsewhere *goy* typically denotes foreign nations while *am* denotes Israel in covenant relationship. Here God applies *goy* to Israel -- the generic term for "nation" -- precisely to contrast Israel's holiness (*qadosh*, "set apart, consecrated") with the nations among whom Israel is one. Israel is a *goy*, a nation like other nations, but *qadosh* -- separated, consecrated, devoted wholly to God. All three translators agree on "kingdom of priests" and "holy nation." The consensus preserves both phrases without modification. LXX: *basileion hierateuma kai ethnos hagion* -- the key divergence is *basileion hierateuma* for *mamlechet kohanim*; the consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Vayyavo Mosheh vayyiqra leziqqney ha'am*: Moses convenes the elders** -- Hebrew: *vayyavo Mosheh vayyiqra leziqqney ha'am vayyasem lifneyhem et kol-haddevarim ha'elleh asher tsivvahu YHWH*. Moses does not go directly to all the people but conveys YHWH's words through the elders -- the mediated structure of covenant communication. The verb *vayyasem* ("he set/placed") before them suggests deliberate, careful presentation rather than casual transmission. EMET renders "he set before them." LOGOS renders "he set before them." PNEUMA renders "laid before them." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "he set before them all these words which YHWH had commanded him."

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Kol asher-dibber YHWH na'aseh*: "All that YHWH has spoken we will do" -- the covenant acceptance** -- Hebrew: *vayyaanu khol-ha'am yachdav vayyomeru kol asher-dibber YHWH na'aseh vayyashev Mosheh et-divrey ha'am el-YHWH*. The word *yachdav* ("together, in unison") echoes the singular *vayyichan* of v. 2 -- the same unity that marked the encampment marks the response. The declaration *na'aseh* ("we will do") is a first-person plural imperfect expressing commitment to action. Israel does not say "we understand" or "we agree" but "we will *do*." The Hebrew emphasis is on performance, not comprehension. In 24:7 the formula expands to *na'aseh venishma* ("we will do and we will hear") -- doing precedes understanding. Moses then functions as the go-between, returning (*vayyashev*, from *shuv*) the people's words to YHWH. Every word of the covenant passes through the mediator. All three translators agree on "we will do" for *na'aseh*. The consensus follows LOGOS for the formal rendering. LXX: *poiesomen* ("we will do") -- preserving the active, performative character.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Be'av he'anan*: "in the thickness of the cloud" -- revelation and concealment** -- Hebrew: *hinneh anokhi ba elekha be'av he'anan ba'avur yishma ha'am bedabberi immakh vegam-bekha ya'aminu le'olam vayyagged Mosheh et-divrey ha'am el-YHWH*. The phrase *be'av he'anan* ("in the thickness of the cloud") uses *av* ("thick, dense") to modify *anan* ("cloud") -- not a wispy cloud but an opaque, impenetrable mass. The cloud simultaneously reveals presence (God is *here*) and conceals form (God cannot be *seen*). The stated purpose is twofold: (1) the people will hear God speaking with Moses, authenticating his prophetic authority; (2) they will trust Moses forever (*ya'aminu le'olam*). EMET renders "in a thickness of the cloud." LOGOS renders "in the thickness of the cloud." PNEUMA renders "in a thick cloud." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "in the thickness of the cloud" -- preserving the construct *av he'anan* rather than PNEUMA's simplified "thick cloud." The final clause, "And Moses told the words of the people to YHWH," is problematic -- the people's words were already reported in v. 8. Many scholars see this as a resumptive repetition or evidence of source combining. The consensus preserves the MT reading. LXX: *en stulo nephelous* ("in a pillar of cloud") -- harmonizing with the pillar-of-cloud motif elsewhere; the MT preserves the distinct term.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Veqiddashtam hayyom umachar*: "consecrate them today and tomorrow" -- preparation for theophany** -- Hebrew: *lekh el-ha'am veqiddashtam hayyom umachar vekibbsu simlotam*. The verb *qiddesh* (Piel of *qadash*, "to consecrate, make holy") means to actively prepare someone for contact with the sacred. The washing of garments is the outward sign of inner preparation. The two-day preparation, with encounter on the third day, creates a liminal period of anticipation. The third-day pattern resonates through biblical narrative (Jonah 1:17; Hos. 6:2). EMET renders "sanctify." LOGOS/PNEUMA render "consecrate." The consensus renders "consecrate" -- the more standard English term for *qiddesh* in ritual contexts.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Yered YHWH le'eyney kol-ha'am al-har Sinay*: "YHWH will come down...upon Mount Sinai" -- the promised descent** -- Hebrew: *vehayu nekhonim layyom hashlishi ki bayyom hashlishi yered YHWH le'eyney kol-ha'am al-har Sinay*. The verb *yered* ("will come down") is the inverse of Moses' *alah* ("going up") -- the meeting happens because both parties move toward each other. The phrase *le'eyney kol-ha'am* ("before the eyes of all the people") establishes this as a public, witnessed event. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Mot yumat*: "shall surely be put to death" -- the death-penalty formula** -- Hebrew: *vehigbalta et-ha'am saviv le'mor hishshamru lakhem alot bahar unegoa beqatsehu kol-hannogea bahar mot yumat*. The verb *vehigbalta* (from *gaval*, "to set a boundary") creates a sacred perimeter. The mountain has become *qadosh* -- set apart, dangerous to the unprepared. The phrase *mot yumat* (Hophal imperfect with infinitive absolute) is the standard formula for the death penalty in Torah: "dying he shall die" -- the passive voice places execution under communal obligation. Touching even the mountain's *qatseh* ("edge, border") is lethal. Holiness in the Hebrew Bible is not merely a moral category but an ontological one -- the divine presence is life-giving at the right distance, lethal at the wrong one. EMET renders "shall surely be put to death." LOGOS renders "shall surely be put to death." PNEUMA renders "will certainly be put to death." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "shall surely be put to death" -- preserving the modal "shall" and the intensifier "surely" which together capture the infinitive absolute construction.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Saqol yissaqel o yaroh yiyyareh...bimshokh hayyovel*: twin infinitive absolutes and the ram's horn signal** -- Hebrew: *lo-tigga bo yad ki-saqol yissaqel o-yaroh yiyyareh im-behemah im-ish lo yichyeh bimshokh hayyovel hemmah ya'alu vahar*. Three features require comment. (1) The stipulation that the offender must not be touched (*lo-tigga bo yad*, "no hand shall touch him") indicates that boundary-violation contamination is contagious; the trespasser must be killed at a distance. (2) The twin infinitive-absolute constructions *saqol yissaqel* ("he shall surely be stoned") and *yaroh yiyyareh* ("he shall surely be shot through") specify execution methods that accomplish death without physical contact. (3) The final clause -- *bimshokh hayyovel* ("when the ram's horn sounds a long blast") -- uses *yovel* ("ram's horn"), the same word that gives the Jubilee year its name (Lev. 25:10). The verb *mashakh* ("to draw out, extend") indicates a sustained blast signaling the transition from restriction to access. EMET renders "surely be stoned or surely shot through...when the yovel sounds a long blast." LOGOS renders "surely be stoned or surely be shot through...when the ram's horn sounds a long blast." PNEUMA renders "stoned or shot through with arrows...when the ram's horn sounds a long blast." The consensus follows LOGOS -- preserving the infinitive absolute construction ("surely be stoned or surely be shot through") and rendering *yovel* as "ram's horn." PNEUMA's addition of "with arrows" is interpretive (the Hebrew *yaroh yiyyareh* means "shot through" without specifying projectiles).

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Vayeqaddesh et-ha'am vayekabbsu simlotam*: Moses consecrates the people** -- All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS: "he consecrated the people, and they washed their garments."

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Al-tiggashu el-ishah*: "do not go near a woman" -- sexual abstinence as consecration** -- Hebrew: *heyu nekhonim lishloshset yamim al-tiggashu el-ishah*. The verb *nagash* ("to approach, draw near") is a euphemism for sexual contact. This instruction -- directed at the men -- requires abstinence as part of ritual preparation. Seminal emission rendered a person temporarily ritually unclean (Lev. 15:16-18). The androcentrism of the address has been noted by scholars: the women are present (v. 3's "house of Jacob") but the preparation instructions assume a male subject. EMET renders "do not approach a woman." LOGOS renders "do not go near a woman." PNEUMA renders "Do not go near a woman." The consensus follows LOGOS. The rendering *lishloshset yamim* as "for the third day" (LOGOS) rather than "for three days" (PNEUMA) is debated; the consensus renders "for the third day" following the Hebrew *lishloshset yamim* which can mean either, but in context refers to readiness for the appointed third day.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Qolot uveraqim*: "thunders and lightnings" -- the sensory overload of theophany** -- Hebrew: *vayehi bayyom hashlishi bihyot habboqer vayyehi qolot uveraqim ve'anan kaved al-hahar veqol shofar chazaq me'od vayyecherad kol-ha'am asher bamachaneh*. Five sensory elements converge: (1) *qolot* ("thunders" / "voices" -- the same Hebrew word means both), plural, suggesting multiple overlapping percussions; (2) *veraqim* ("lightnings"), visible flashes; (3) *anan kaved* ("heavy cloud"), using *kaved* (the same root as Pharaoh's hardened heart) -- the cloud has *weight*; (4) *qol shofar chazaq me'od* ("the sound of a horn exceedingly strong") -- the shofar emanates from the theophany itself, not from a human player; (5) *vayyecherad* ("trembled"), the people's bodily response. The consensus renders "thunders and lightnings" rather than "thunder and lightning" (singular) -- preserving the Hebrew plural *qolot...veraqim*. EMET renders "thunders and lightnings." LOGOS renders "thunders and lightnings." PNEUMA renders "thunder and lightning" (singular). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS in preserving the plural forms. LXX: the trumpet becomes *salpigx*, the instrument of eschatological theophany in 1 Thess. 4:16 and 1 Cor. 15:52.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Vayyotse Mosheh et-ha'am liqrat ha'Elohim*: "to meet God" -- assembly for encounter** -- Hebrew: *vayyotse Mosheh et-ha'am liqrat ha'Elohim min-hamachaneh vayyityatsvu betachtit hahar*. The phrase *liqrat ha'Elohim* ("to meet God") is extraordinary -- this is not a casual gathering but a deliberate meeting (*liqrat*, from *qara*, "to encounter, to meet face-to-face"). The verb *vayyityatsvu* ("they stationed themselves") implies ordered, intentional positioning. EMET renders "at the base of the mountain." LOGOS renders "at the foot of the mountain." PNEUMA renders "at the foot of the mountain." The consensus renders "at the foot of the mountain" -- following LOGOS/PNEUMA for idiomatic English, though EMET's "base" is equally valid for *betachtit*.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Yarad alav YHWH ba'esh...ke'ashan hakkivshan*: "YHWH descended upon it in fire...like the smoke of a kiln"** -- Hebrew: *vehar Sinay ashan kullo mippeney asher yarad alav YHWH ba'esh vayya'al ashano ke'ashan hakkivshan vayyecherad kol-hahar me'od*. The fire (*esh*) is the primary visible manifestation of YHWH's presence, as at the burning bush (3:2). The comparison *ke'ashan hakkivshan* ("like smoke of a kiln") uses *kivshan* ("kiln, smelting furnace") -- the same word associated with the furnace of Egyptian brick-making. The imagery deliberately recalls both the Abrahamic covenant vision (Gen. 15:17) and the furnace of slavery. The mountain itself trembles -- *vayyecherad kol-hahar* -- the same verb (*charad*) applied to the people in v. 16. When God descends, everything shakes. EMET renders "all of it smoking...like the smoke of a kiln...the whole mountain trembled exceedingly." LOGOS renders "was all of it smoking...like the smoke of a kiln...trembled greatly." PNEUMA renders "was wrapped entirely in smoke...billowed up like smoke from a kiln...shook violently." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "was all of it smoking" preserves *ashan kullo* more literally than PNEUMA's "wrapped entirely in smoke." LXX reads "all the people trembled" (*pas ho laos*) rather than "the whole mountain" (*kol-hahar*), shifting the locus of trembling from nature to humanity; the consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Holekh vechazaq me'od...ya'annennu beqol*: the crescendo and the divine answer** -- Hebrew: *vayehi qol hashshofar holekh vechazaq me'od Mosheh yedabber veha'Elohim ya'annennu beqol*. The participle construction *holekh vechazaq* ("going and strengthening") describes a trumpet that never stops increasing -- unlike a human-blown instrument, which fades, this shofar intensifies without limit. The final word *beqol* is ambiguous: "with a voice" (intelligible speech) or "in thunder" (overwhelming sound). The text preserves both possibilities. The consensus renders "with a voice" -- following all three translators, though the footnote preserves the ambiguity. EMET renders "with a voice." LOGOS renders "with a voice." PNEUMA renders "in thunder." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "with a voice" -- which captures the primary sense of *beqol*; PNEUMA's "in thunder" is an interpretive rendering, valid but secondary.

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Vayyered YHWH al-har Sinay el-rosh hahar*: the descent to the summit** -- All three translators agree. The consensus follows LOGOS: "YHWH came down upon Mount Sinai, to the top of the mountain; and YHWH called Moses to the top of the mountain, and Moses went up."

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Pen-yehersu el-YHWH lir'ot*: "lest they break through to YHWH to gaze"** -- Hebrew: *red ha'ed ba'am pen-yehersu el-YHWH lir'ot venaphal mimmennu rav*. The verb *yehersu* (from *haras*, "to break down, tear through") is violent -- not merely approaching but breaching, smashing through the boundary. The spectacle is so overwhelming that people might forget themselves. The infinitive *lir'ot* ("to gaze, to see") suggests the danger of voyeuristic curiosity about the divine form. EMET renders "lest they break through...to see." LOGOS renders "lest they break through...to gaze." PNEUMA renders "not to force their way through to look at YHWH." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "lest they break through to YHWH to gaze" -- preserving the Hebrew word order and the stronger verb "gaze" for *lir'ot* in this context.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Pen-yifrats bahem YHWH*: "lest YHWH break out against them" -- the hydraulic metaphor of holiness** -- Hebrew: *vegam hakkohanim hannigashim el-YHWH yitqaddashu pen-yifrats bahem YHWH*. The verb *yifrats* (from *parats*, "to break out, burst forth") depicts YHWH's holiness as a contained force that can erupt. The metaphor is volcanic or hydraulic -- divine holiness under pressure, released catastrophically upon contact with the unconsecrated. This language recurs at the death of Uzzah (2 Sam. 6:8). The identity of these pre-Sinai "priests" is debated; the Levitical priesthood has not yet been established (Exod. 28-29). These may be the firstborn sons, who held priestly functions before the Levites were designated (Num. 3:12-13). All three translators agree on "break out against them." The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- Moses' objection: the boundary is already set** -- Hebrew: *lo-yukhal ha'am la'alot el-har Sinay ki-attah ha'edotah banu le'mor hagbel et-hahar veqiddashtoh*. Moses' response -- "you yourself warned us" -- seems to challenge YHWH's instruction to go down and warn again. Some scholars see this as evidence of source-splicing; others as emphasizing the urgency of the danger. Even with boundaries set, the gravitational pull of theophany might draw people to breach them. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Lekh red ve'alita attah ve'Aharon immakh*: "Go, descend, and come up -- you and Aaron with you"** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer YHWH elav lekh red ve'alita attah ve'Aharon immakh vehakohanim veha'am al-yehersu la'alot el-YHWH pen-yifrats bam*. Aaron's first appearance in the Sinai narrative -- he is singled out as the one person who may accompany Moses, while the priests and people are barred. This anticipates Aaron's later priestly role. The double imperative "go, descend" (*lekh red*) conveys urgency. EMET renders "Go, go down, and you shall come up -- you and Aharon with you." LOGOS renders "Go, descend, and come up, you and Aaron with you." PNEUMA renders "Go down. Then come back up -- you and Aaron with you." The consensus follows LOGOS for the formal rendering, using "descend" for *red* and preserving the Hebrew command structure.

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Vayyered Mosheh el-ha'am vayyomer alehem*: the abrupt ending** -- Hebrew: *vayyered Mosheh el-ha'am vayyomer alehem*. This final verse is strikingly incomplete: "And Moses went down to the people and said to them" -- said *what*? The verse trails off without reporting the content of Moses' speech. The chapter ends mid-sentence, propelling the reader directly into chapter 20 and the Decalogue. The effect is literary suspension: Moses opens his mouth to repeat the warnings, but before we hear his words, God's voice thunders from the mountain with the Ten Words (20:1). Many scholars see this as a deliberate editorial decision -- the narrator suppresses Moses' speech because it is immediately superseded by God's own voice. Others see it as evidence of redactional seaming between source documents. Either way, the abruptness is powerful: Moses goes down, begins to speak, and then -- God speaks. The LXX also ends abruptly, preserving the MT's suspension: *katebe de Mouses pros ton laon kai eipen autois* ("and Moses went down to the people and said to them"). All three translators preserve the abrupt ending. The consensus follows the MT without addition or harmonization.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
